quiz.title: Unit 2 Quiz 12, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Orbitals, Principles, and Electron Configuration Review
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: s, p, d Orbitals, Aufbau Principle, Hund's Rule, Pauli Exclusion Principle, Noble Gas Configurations, Unpaired Electrons, Energy Level Ordering

questions.question: One of these is not true about s orbitals:
options: They are found in all principal energy levels
They can only hold one electron
The maximum number of s orbitals in any principal level is 1
questions.explanation: s orbitals can hold a maximum of two electrons (with opposite spins), not one. All other statements are correct.

questions.question: One of these is not true about p orbitals:
options: They are found in all principal energy levels
There are three types of p orbital
Each p orbital can hold up to two electrons
questions.explanation: p orbitals are NOT found in all principal energy levels. They first appear in the second principal energy level (n=2). The first energy level (n=1) only has an s orbital.

questions.question: According to Aufbau principle:
options: Only two electrons can occupy an orbital
Electrons enter the lowest available energy level
Orbitals are regions in space where one is likely to find an electron
questions.explanation: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals starting from the lowest available energy level before moving to higher ones. Option A is the Pauli exclusion principle, option C is Hund's rule, and option D is the definition of an orbital.

questions.question: One of the following about d orbitals is NOT TRUE:
options: They are not found in the first two principal energy levels
They are associated with transition elements
There are 5 types of d orbital
d orbitals are filled before p orbitals in the same principal energy level
questions.explanation: Within the same principal energy level, p orbitals fill before d orbitals. For example, in n=3, the order is 3s → 3p → 3d. d orbitals do not appear until n=3.

questions.question: One of the following is FALSE:
options: The 3d sublevel is filled before the 4s sublevel
The 3rd principal energy level only contains 8 electrons
Principal energy levels get closer together as they get further from the nucleus
Orbitals are always filled in numerical order
questions.explanation: The 4s sublevel fills BEFORE the 3d sublevel, not after. 4s has lower energy than 3d. Option B is false as well (3rd level can hold 18 electrons), but A is the clearest violation of Aufbau order.

questions.question: All of the following represent a noble gas, EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: 1s² 2s² represents beryllium (Z=4), which is not a noble gas. 1s² is helium, 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ is neon, and 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ is argon — all noble gases.

questions.question: According to Hund's rule:
options: Electrons enter orbitals of the same energy singly before pairing
Electrons enter the lowest available energy level
An orbital can hold up to two electrons
No two electrons can have the same set of four quantum numbers
questions.explanation: Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity states that for degenerate orbitals (orbitals with the same energy, such as the three p-orbitals), electrons will occupy them singly with parallel spins before they begin to pair up. This minimizes electron-electron repulsion and results in a more stable, lower-energy state for the atom. Think of it like passengers on a bus: people usually take an empty double seat before sitting next to someone else.

questions.question: For a chlorine atom, the K, L, M shell distribution is:
questions.explanation: Chlorine (Cl, Z=17) has electron configuration 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁵. This gives K shell (n=1) = 2 electrons, L shell (n=2) = 8 electrons, and M shell (n=3) = 7 electrons. The distribution is 2, 8, 7.

questions.question: How many valence electrons does an element with atomic number 16 have?
questions.explanation: Atomic number 16 is sulfur (S). Its electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁴. The valence electrons are those in the outermost shell (n=3), which are 3s² and 3p⁴, totaling 6 valence electrons.

questions.question: Which of the following has two unpaired electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxygen (1s² 2s² 2p⁴) has two unpaired electrons in the 2p subshell. Nitrogen has three unpaired, neon has zero, and beryllium has zero.

questions.question: Which element has atoms with the greatest number of singly occupied orbitals?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen (N, Z=7) has the electron configuration 1s² 2s² 2p³. According to Hund's rule, the three 2p electrons each occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins, giving nitrogen three singly occupied orbitals. Carbon has two, fluorine has one, and magnesium has zero.

questions.question: Which of the following has three unpaired electrons?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen (1s² 2s² 2p³) has three unpaired electrons in the 2p subshell, each in a separate orbital with parallel spins according to Hund's rule.

questions.question: Which of the following has one unpaired electron?
questions.explanation: Sodium (1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹) has one unpaired electron in the 3s orbital. Oxygen has two unpaired, nitrogen has three, magnesium has zero.

questions.question: What is the electronic configuration of an oxide ion O²⁻?
questions.explanation: Oxygen (Z=8) gains two electrons to form O²⁻, giving it 10 electrons. The configuration becomes 1s² 2s² 2p⁶, which is the same as neon (isoelectronic).

questions.question: Why do electrons enter the 4s sublevel before the 3d sublevel?
options: There is only one 4s orbital and there are five 3d orbitals
The 4s orbital has a lower energy
The 3d orbitals have a lower energy
questions.explanation: Electrons fill the lowest available energy level first. The 4s orbital has lower energy than 3d, so it fills first. This is due to penetration effects and the (n+l) rule.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered orbital theory and electron configuration principles! ⚛️🎯
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the properties of s, p, and d orbitals and the differences between Aufbau, Hund, and Pauli.
scoreMessages.50: Fair effort. Focus on unpaired electrons and energy level ordering.
scoreMessages.0: Please review orbital shapes, electron configuration rules, and the principles that govern electron filling.
